coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of magnetic graphene quantum dots based molecularly imprinted polymers for fluorescent determination of quercetin
coredata.dc:creator: Mantashloo, Roghayeh
Bahar, Soleiman
coredata.dc:description: In this work, modified magnetic graphene quantum dots with molecularly imprinted polymer coating (Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP) through the self-assembly reaction of APTES (functional monomer), TEOS (cross-linker) and quercetin (template) molecules as a low-cost new green adsorbent with high selectivity and sensitivity for easy evaluation of quercetin medicinal compound using the quenching effect of quercetin on the fluorescence emission intensity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized. The morphology and composition of GQDs, magnetic graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4@GQDs) and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) techniques were determined. The regular and uniform distribution of spherical nanoparticles of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP was well observed by SEM imagings. In the next part of this project, the effective parameters in the process of measuring quercetin such as concentration of adsorbent, pH and contact time were investigated, that the optimal values of concentration of adsorbent 0.6 mg mL−1, pH 8.0 and contact time 10 min were obtained. In the optimized conditions, quercetin with a linear range of 5–220 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.54 ng mL−1 and recoveries of 97.61–102.11(%) and repeatability of 1.18–3.71(%) in real samples of green tea, cumin and thyme was successfully measured and detected.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetit nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Quercetin
Fluorescenc

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical application of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP for the detection of quercetin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the University of Kurdistan for all the support it has provided in all aspects of the above project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EDAX spectra for (a) GQDs (b) Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra for GQDs, (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra for quercetin and (e) fluorescence emission spectra for GQDs using different excitation wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra for (a) GQDs, (b) Fe3O4@GQDs, (c) Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP and (d-g) Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP (after and before washing), (B) FT-IR spectra for (a) GQDs, (b) Fe3O4@GQDs, (c, d) Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP (before and after washing) and (e) Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP.

Effect of (a(concentration of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, (b) pH [15 min contact time, 0.6 mg mL−1 concentration of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP], (c(contact time [0.6 mg mL−1 concentration of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, pH 8.0] for the detection of 100 ng mL−1 quercetin and (d) Fluorescence emission calibration diagram of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP for different concentrations of quercetin in the concentration range of 5–220 ng mL−1 [10 min contact time, concentration of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP 0.6 mg mL−1, pH 8.0, λex = 350 nm ].

(a) Effect of some compounds (including quercetin-like structures and some ions) on the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP response to determine quercetin, in the presence or absence of quercetin (10 min contact time, concentration of adsorbent 0.6 mg mL−1, pH 8.0, λex= 350 nm); (b) Adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP for quercetin, gallic acid and caffeine molecules.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP (before and after washing) by the proposed method..
Comparison of the proposed method for determination of quercetin using Fe3O4/GQDs/MIP with other adsorbent.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a Graphitic carbon nitride nanoparticle/ molecularly imprinted polymer. / b Metal-organic framework compound comprised of NH2-UiO-66 and ZIF-8. / c Nitrogen, sulfur doped carbon dots. / d Zinc sulfur-quantum dots. / e Sulfur doped graphene quantum dots. / f Fe3O4 / graphene quantum dots. /molecularly imprinted polymer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tolerance limit for some interference species in the determination of quercetin (100 ng mL−1) using fluorescence method (in optimum condition).
Selectivity parameters for quercetin, gallic acid and caffeine.
The results of quercetin measurement in real samples, before and after spiking with quercetin using Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP adsorbent by fluorescence method.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of magnetic graphene quantum dots based molecularly imprinted polymers for fluorescent determination of quercetin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Kurdistan, P.O. Box 416 Sanandaj, I.R. Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Kurdistan, P.O. Box 416 Sanandaj, I.R. Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: at: University of Kurdistan, Department of Chemistry, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, modified magnetic graphene quantum dots with molecularly imprinted polymer coating (Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP) through the self-assembly reaction of APTES (functional monomer), TEOS (cross-linker) and quercetin (template) molecules as a low-cost new green adsorbent with high selectivity and sensitivity for easy evaluation of quercetin medicinal compound using the quenching effect of quercetin on the fluorescence emission intensity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized. The morphology and composition of GQDs, magnetic graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4@GQDs) and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) techniques were determined. The regular and uniform distribution of spherical nanoparticles of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP was well observed by SEM imagings. In the next part of this project, the effective parameters in the process of measuring quercetin such as concentration of adsorbent, pH and contact time were investigated, that the optimal values of concentration of adsorbent 0.6 mg mL−1, pH 8.0 and contact time 10 min were obtained. In the optimized conditions, quercetin with a linear range of 5–220 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.54 ng mL−1 and recoveries of 97.61–102.11(%) and repeatability of 1.18–3.71(%) in real samples of green tea, cumin and thyme was successfully measured and detected.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Flavonoids are one of the largest families of nutrients known to scientists. This nutrient group is more famous for having antioxidants and anti-inflammatories. The name of this plant nutrient group is actually derived from the chemistry related to their color, which is the Latin word flavus meaning “yellow”. However, as a group, flavonoids are highly bioactive compounds and play a variety of different roles in the health of plants, animals and human health [1–8]. Most flavonoids are flavonols and among them, quercetin 3,3ʹ,4ʹ,5,7-pentahydroxy flavonol (Fig. S1), is famous [9–11]. The molecular weight of quercetin is 302.24, which is slightly soluble in water and ethanol and in large quantities in acetic acid and alkaline solutions. Quercetin molecules have several hydroxyl groups adjacent to benzene rings (pKa 6.90), that can form complexes with metal ions that prevent them from being present in oxidation processes. The mentioned property, together with the ease of oxidation of quercetin and the ability of phenolic remainders to separate radicals that are produced, determines the high antioxidant capacity of quercetin. Quercetin has anti-inflammatory, anti-histamine, anti-swelling, anti-viral and anti-cancer effects, stabilizes cell membranes, inhibits the aging process of the skin, cornea and myocardium, and has a positive effect on the activity of the cardiovascular and liver system [12–16]. The daily dose of quercetin has been reported from 4 to 68 mg [13]. Since plants and spices rich in polyphenols have valuable properties [17], creating a selective, sensitive, accurate and low-cost superior sensor or adsorbent by analytical method for extraction, measurement and quantification of quercetin is very important and necessary. The various analysis methods such as mass spectrometry [18], liquid chromatography [19], UV–vis spectrophotometry [20] and electrochemical methods [21] have been used to determination of quercetin.

Quantum dots (QDs) are fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals at the nanometer scale (size < 5 nm) that have special electrical and optical properties such as high quantum efficiency, adjustable size, narrow scattering, long-term light stability, luminescence, emissions Stimulus-dependent, photo-bleaching resistance, and surface correction compatibility [22,23]. The electronic optical properties of QDs are due to their semiconductor nature. Recently, a new type of QDs, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), has attracted a lot of attention. GQDs are zero-dimensional materials that appear as derivatives of graphene properties and carbon dots (CDs), so they can be thought of as one, two, or more layers of very small graphene fragments. Precisely ideal GQDs have a single carbon-only atomic layer that exhibits unusual optical properties due to the quantum constraint effect of conjugate π amplitudes and the edge effect. GQDs have many advantages over semiconductor QDs, like resistant fluorescence [24–26], little toxicity [27–29] and suitable water solubility [30,31]. Among these properties, the fluorescence emission is the most prominent property of GQDs.

One of the greatest concerns of all scientists, is to mimic biological functions, such as enzymes and antibodies because success in it promises to enrich our daily lives. Technology is one of the ways used to access artificial molecular recognition. Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) is today a synthetic approach suitable for the design of molecular diagnostic materials that can mimic natural diagnostic entities, such as antibodies and biological receptors. With the ever-increasing progress and many applications in analytical chemistry, MIT is faced with the challenge of complying with the requirements of green sustainable development. Recognizing the environmental risks associated with unsustainable MIT, a new aspect of MIT, called green MIT, has emerged and developed [32–36].

Molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) are highly cross-linked organic polymers made from silica-based materials with easy polymerization. A combination of target molecules (templates) and functional monomers that are attached to the templates through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions to form a pre-polymerization complex. A crosslinking monomer is then added to the mixture in the presence of a primer and a porous solvent to form a stable tridimensional (3D) cross-sectional complex. The synthesized MIP represents a porous polymer matrix with specific identification sites available for a particular template or analyte that enables the MIP to selectively bind this template molecule (or its analogues). Moreover, compared to natural samples, MIPs can remain stable under harsh conditions including high temperature and pressure, strong acid/base, and organic solvents. For these outstanding features such as predictable structure, selective detection and universal applicability, MIPs have attracted much attention and played an important role in analytical studies [37].

As an important magnetic material, magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) have attracted increasing attention for use in sensors and adsorbents due to their high surface area, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy preparation, and high adsorption capacity. They can save time and energy and eliminate the need for centrifuges and filtration systems [38,39]. Fe3O4 NPs, as the name suggests, can interact with a magnetic field to make use of their application in the sample preparation process because of their superparamagnetic property, they are attracted by a magnet but do not retain after the removal of the magnetic field.

Smart materials or multifunctional materials with physical or chemical properties that sense changes in the environment and respond to external stimuli give more flexibility to the development of new analytical methods that reduce the amount of waste. Using various external stimuli (light, magnetic and electric fields, temperature, pH of the environment, etc.) has been tested in several applications, mainly analytical and bioanalytical, leading to increased flexibility and economy, as well as higher selectivity and sensitivity of the analysis.

MIPs and GQDs are a type of smart materials in green analytical chemistry (GAC) that have many applications. Greening in analytical chemistry is mainly about reducing the use of solvents and harmful chemicals. GAC is a part of the concept of sustainable development, miniaturization of analyzers and shortening the time elapsed between performing an analysis and obtaining reliable analytical results are important aspects of green analytical chemistry [40].

In this work, Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP were prepared by simple method and used to measure quercetin in plant samples. The synthesis process was fast, simple, green and economical. GQDs was first prepared by thermal decomposition (pyrolysis) of glucose precursor and then after magnetization was coated with a layer of MIP. Quercetin was selected as the templet imprinting for the creation of receiving sites in the polymer surface. After effective washing to remove the templet molecules, the residual imprinting site acted as a special acceptor and had the ability to absorb quercetin from soluble samples with appropriate selectivity. The presence of quercetin causes the quenching of fluorescence emission due to the creation of molecular interactions with Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and disturbance in the electronic properties of GQD. According to the Stern-Volmer equation (Fo/F = 1 + Ksv⋅[C]), fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with concentration. Therefore, we can use the drop of the fluorescence emission signal (ΔF) for quantitative measurement quercetin. The figure summarized in Scheme 1 shows the synthesis process of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP (before and after washing) by the proposed method.

A scanning electron microscope equipped with X-ray diffraction (FESEM & EDAX) spectroscopy of MIRA3 TESCAN HV model was used to study the structure and morphology of the compounds. FT-IR spectra were recorded by a spectrophotometer model Vector-22 manufactured by Bruker (Switzerland) and UV–vis spectra were recorded by a spectrophotometer model SPECTROD 250-analytikjena (Germany). Also, VSM spectra were taken by VSM + FORC model vibration sample magnetometer (made in Iran, Kashan Daneshpajooh Magnet Company). Fluorescence spectra were also obtained by the CARY Eclipse model (manufactured by Applied Science, Iran). Quercetin measurement and data recording was performed using a JENWAY 6285 fluorimeter (made in England).

The chemicals and reagents used such as glucose, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), methanol, ethanol, ethylene glycol, solution ammonia (25 %), iron (III) chloride (FeCl3·6H2O), anhydrous sodium acetate, and trisodium citrate dihydrate were purchased from Merck (Germany). Quercetin, gallic acid and caffeine were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich company. Also, to prepare a solution of quercetin reserves (with a concentration of 100 μg mL−1), appropriate amounts of pure quercetin powder were dissolved in deionized water.

In the present work, we have used the carbonization method to synthesize GQDs. First, 2 g of pure glucose powder was melted at 180 °C for 30 min using the pyrolysis process, which resulted in the formation of a dark orange solution, indicating the formation of GQD. Then the off-heating source and 8.3 mL of deionized water were added to the above solution under continuous and intense stirring to form a solution of GQDs [41].

We used the solvothermal method for the synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs. For this purpose, 2.7 g of FeCl3·6H2O in 80 mL of ethylene glycol in a glass beaker was stirred vigorously with a magnetic stirrer to dissolve. Then 6.2 g of anhydrous sodium acetate and 0.8 g of trisodium sodium citrate were added to the mixture and stirred vigorously for 1 h in an oil bath at 150 °C. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL autoclave with Teflon coating and placed in an oven at 200 °C for 6 h to react. After the reaction, the brown Fe3O4 NPs were collected using an external magnetic field and washed several times with ethanol solution to remove excess reactants. The precipitate was then dried for 24 h in a vacuum oven at 40 °C. Finally, 5 mg of Fe3O4 NPs were mixed with 2 mL of GQD solution and dispersed under ultrasound for 10 min to synthesize of the Fe3O4@GQDs solution [42].

To coat the surface of Fe3O4@GQDs with MIP, first 500 μL of Fe3O4@GQDs solution, 0.02 mmol of quercetin as a templet molecule, 0.08 mmol of 3-aminopropyl ethoxy silane solution (APTES) as the functional monomer and 10 mL of ethanol solution as the porous solvent were mixed together. The above solution was then stirred for 1 h at room temperature with a magnetic stirrer with a rotation speed of 700 rpm. After 1 h, 0.4 mmol of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as a crosslinker and 300 μL of 25 % ammonia solution as a polymerization initiator was added to the solution and stirred at room temperature for 12 h until synthesize Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP. After some time, Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP was isolated by an external magnetic field and rinsed several times with distilled water to remove excess reactants. Then, Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP was washed several times with methanol solvent to remove the templet molecules and create a hole in the MIP. This process was continued until the adsorption of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP supernatant was zero. Finally, the synthesized adsorbent it was dried in an oven at 40 °C for 4 h. Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP (non– imprinted polymers) was synthesized in the same way but in the absence of the template molecule [43].

To determine of quercetin through fluorescence, a constant dispersed amount (0.6 mg mL−1, 300 μL) of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP is typically mixed with a quercetin solution sample (100 ng mL−1, 300 μL, pH 8.0). The prepared mixture was stirred for 10 min to absorb the quercetin on the adsorbent. Finally, the stirring was stopped and the fluorescence intensity was written at 456 nm (λex = 350 nm).

The used real samples were green tea (Iran, Lahijan), thyme and cumin which were collected from the plains of Kurdistan province. They were washed and dried in a dark environment at 25 °C and then milled to obtain the desired powder sample. For preparation of tea sample, first 1 g of the tea sample was added to 30 mL of boiling water and after 5 min, the resulting mixture was centrifuged for 10 min and passed through the filter paper twice in order to achieve an aqueous extraction solution of tea [44]. For preparation thyme and cumin samples, first, 5 g of each sample was extracted with the desired solvent (which included 12.5 mL of ethanol, 12.5 mL of deionized water and 25 μL of formic acid). They were then subjected to ultrasound for 5 min and then centrifuged for 10 min. The supernatant was placed inside a rotary apparatus to remove the organic solvent during the evaporation process. Finally, after removing the solvent, the obtained extracts were made up to volume with 25 mL of water containing 25 μL of formic acid. The extracts were stored at 4 °C until testing and analysis [45].

As shown in the summery image in Scheme 1, the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP were prepared in a simple method and used to determination of quercetin in plant samples. The synthesis process was fast, simple, green and economical. GQDs was first prepared by thermal decomposition (pyrolysis) of glucose precursor and after magnetization, Fe3O4@GQDs was coated with a layer of MIP. Quercetin was selected as the template molecule for the creation of receiving sites in the polymer surface. After effective washing to remove the template molecules, the residual imprinting sites acted as a special acceptor and were able to adsorb quercetin from sample with appropriate selectivity. The presence of quercetin causes the quenching of fluorescence emission due to the creation of molecular interactions with Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and disturbance in the electronic properties of GQD. According to the Stern-Volmer equation (Fo/F = 1 + Ksv [C]), fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with concentration. Therefore, we can use the drop of the fluorescence emission signal (ΔF) for quantitative measurement quercetin.

The morphology of GQD, Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP were investigated by SEM. As can be seen from the images, GQD particles have a uniform, dot-shaped morphology that indicates the crystalline behavior of the GQD layers. GQDs also have two-dimensional layered structures in the form of folded sheets (most GQDs are composed of several graphene layers). The presence of aggregated particles is probably due to the accumulation of graphene sheets (Fig. S2a). SEM image of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP samples are shown in Fig. S2b and Fig. S2c respectively. As shown in Fig. 2, the MIP particles have spherical, fine, homogeneous and regular surfaces with uniform distribution, which confirms the successful formation of the MIP layer on the Fe3O4@GQDs surface. Also, the small size of the MIP particles increases the surface area to the volume of the particles and thus increases the adsorption capacity of analyte molecules in the MIP sites and fast binding kinetics because the binding sites are exposed to the surface. MIP surfaces are rougher than NIP surfaces, in addition to the non-uniform distribution, irregular, heterogeneous and large size of NIP particles, it shows the obvious structural difference between MIP and NIP, which indicates the correct process of imprinting quercetin molecules in the MIP adsorbent.

The structure of GQD and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP were investigated by EDAX technique. The EDAX spectra of the GQDs and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP was recorded to identify the type and dispersion of elements. (Fig. 1 a, b). The presence of carbon and oxygen confirms the structure of GQD. The presence of the iron element indicates the magnetization of GQDs. The appearance of high-strength silica peaks also confirms the successful formation of a polymer coating on the surface of the Fe3O4@GQDs.

The UV–vis spectra of GQDs was also examined (Fig. 1c). The two closely absorption peaks are related to the electron transitions of the two transitions π-π and n-π of the carbon–carbon double bond in the aromatic ring structure and the carbonyl functional group on the surface of GQDs. As shown in the figure, the maximum absorption signal for the synthesized GQD occurs at 323 nm.

To investigate the effect of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of GQD solution at different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 430 nm was measured by a two-beam spectrofluorimeter (Fig. 1e). As can be seen, the synthesized GQD solution gives the maximum fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 456 nm using the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Therefore, a wavelength of 350 nm was considered as the excitation wavelength and 456 nm considered as the optimal emission wavelength and used in subsequent experiments.

The chemical structure of the synthetic samples was investigated by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 B). The FT -IR peaks of GQDs at wavenumbers of 3420 cm−1 (OH bond), 2926 cm−1 (stretching vibration for CH bond), 1413 cm−1 (bending vibration for CH bond), 1635 cm−1 (CO bond) and 1032 cm−1 (CC bond) were observed. Also, the peak at 624 cm−1 is related to the FeO bond in Fe3O4@GQDs. Strong peak at 1091 cm−1, medium peaks at 799 cm−1 and 468 cm−1 related to asymmetric stretching vibration of the SiO bond, symmetric stretching vibration of the SiO bond and bending vibration of the SiOSi bond respectively, indicates the successful bonding of the silica group in the crosslinking agent TEOS to the surface of Fe3O4@GQDs. The weak peaks at 955 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 indicates asymmetric stretching vibration of the NH bond and the CN bond, and the mean peak at 3422 cm−1 indicates the symmetric stretching vibration of the NH bond in the functional monomer of APTES.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 (curve a) and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP (curve b) samples are determined by VSM analysis (Fig.S3). The amount of saturated magnet for Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP nanoparticles is 61.71 and 7.31 emu/g, respectively. As can be seen from the figure, the curves a and b have a similar shape and symmetry. The decrease in the amount of magnetism for Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP is due to the coating of the surface of Fe3O4 NPs with a thick layer of silica and GQDs. Also, the internal image shown in the figure is of the dispersed solution of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, which is attracted to the magnet in 2 min using its magnetic property. Thus Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP exhibits acceptable and good magnetic properties to facilitate of magnetic separation of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP from solution during the target removal process from MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP for the detection of quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: A selective and specific method for the quercetin molecule was designed using synthesized Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP. The imprinting process was performed using quercetin molecule as the target molecule and in the polymerization process of TEOS (crosslinker), APTES (functional monomer) and GQDs (suitable substrate and fluorescent emission agent) were used. Also, Fe3O4 as magnetic coating was used to facilitate the separation and removal of the template molecule from the molecular imprinting substrate. The elution process was performed to remove quercetin molecules from the polymer with methanol solvent and the completion of the elution process was estimated by the fluorescence emission intensity signal of the MIP (Fig. 2A). The fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP after 6 washing steps was approximately equal to the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP, which indicates the release of quercetin molecules from the mold. The removal of the quercetin molecules was also evaluated by the FT-IR spectra. Weak peaks at the wavenumbers of 1457 cm−1, 1773 cm−1, 2026 cm−1 and 3927 cm−1 in Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP spectra (Fig. 2Bc) are related to stretching vibration (CC) in the aromatic ring, ketone bond (CO), aromatic bond (CC), and the (OH) bond in phenolic compound of quercetin molecule, respectively. These wavenumbers were lost in the spectra after 6 washing steps, which indicates the complete removal of quercetin from MIP (Fig. 2B–d). The similarity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP spectra after washing with Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP spectra (Fig. 2B–e) confirm this claim. Now, the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP absorbent has specific and appropriate empty sites (in terms of shape, size and functional groups) related to the quercetin molecule. As a result, Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP has a great tendency to selectively absorb quercetin molecules. On the other hand, in this complex, GQDs act as a source of light and fluorescence so that they can detect quercetin molecules. Placing quercetin molecules in the MIP places caused a significant quenching effect in the emission intensity of GQD, which is from this effect, can be used for quantitative and selective determination of quercetin in real samples.

Fluorescence emission occurs as a result of photon absorption in the ground state and reaching the excited state. When the electrons return from the excited state to the ground state, the excitation energy is released in the form of fluorescence emission, but in some cases this energy release may be affected and cause fluorescence emission to be quenched. There are various ways to quench fluorescence, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE) [46]. Generally, in the FRET process, there should be a suitable spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of quercetin and the fluorescence emission spectrum of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP for quenching to occur. Fig. 1d and Fig. 2A–d shows that there is little spectral overlap, proving that the quenching mechanism is definitely not FRET. but the IFE mechanism occurs when the absorption spectrum of quercetin overlaps with the excitation spectrum of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP. As seen in Fig. 1d, e there is some spectral overlap, so the fluorescence quenching may be partly due to the IFE mechanism. Also, fluorescence quenching can be done in two ways: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Static quenching is caused by the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between the fluorophores and quencher molecules, but dynamic quenching is caused by collisions between the fluorophores and quencher molecules. When quercetin is added to the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP solution, molecular interactions (such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic force and π-π interactions) between quercetin and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP occur. Then molecular interaction of hydrogen bond type between the amino group of APTES and the hydroxyl group of quercetin is created, which causes the formation of the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP-Quercetin complex. This complex prevents the transfer of non-radiative electron to the hole. Therefore, it can be considered that the electron-hole pair of GQDs can be affected by the adjacent quercetin molecules in MIP sites and the process of transferring excited electrons to the ground state is disturbed. Finally, the energy of photons is transferred to the quercetin molecule and causes static quenching of fluorescence emission. As a result, the quenching mechanism is a combination of static quenching and to some extent the IFE process.

To achieve the optimal conditions and improve the sensitivity of the method, the effective parameters in the process of measuring quercetin in plant samples and medicinal spices by reducing the fluorescence emission signal of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP in the absence and presence of quercetin (ΔF = F0 – F) were investigated. For this purpose, the effect of concentration of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, pH and contact time between quercetin solution and Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP were considered.

To evaluate the effect of concentration of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, the adsorbent solutions with a concentration range of 0.1 to 1.3 mg mL−1 were prepared and then the fluorescence intensities of the samples were recorded by a fluorimeter. As presented in Fig. 3 a, fluorescence intensity at a concentration of 0.6 mg mL−1 of the adsorbent had the highest signal. So that, increasing the concentration of the adsorbent up to 0.6 mg mL−1, the intensity of fluorescence increases (the concentration has a direct and linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity), but at values higher than this concentration, the fluorescence decreases. Because with the increase in the concentration and movement of molecules, the collision and vibration of molecules increases, as a result internal system conversion are strengthened and the fluorescence equation deviates from the experimental state and causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. As a result, we considered the concentration of 0.6 mg mL−1 as the optimal concentration of the adsorbent and used it in the next experiments.

To evaluate the effect of pH, experiments were performed at different pH values in the range of 4 to 12. Then a fluorescence signal (ΔF) was recorded for a certain concentration of quercetin (100 ng mL−1) with a contact time of 15 min (Fig. 3b). The highest fluorescence intensity drop (ΔF) was obtained at pH 8.0. In acidic pH values, due to the repulsion of the positive charge of the analyte due to protonation with the positive charge of the NH2 group on the surface of the polymer, analyte molecules cannot interact properly with the active sites (shaped holes) in the polymer (due to electrostatic, hydrogen bonding or non-covalent interactions). Therefore, the amount of quercetin absorption decreases and increases the fluorescence intensity (at least ΔF). But gradually with the increase of pH and approaching the pKa of quercetin (pKa: 6.9), the amount of quercetin absorption increases and reaches its highest level at pH 8.0, because by losing a proton and creating a partial negative charge on the surface of the analyte molecules and creating an attractive force with a positive charge on the surface of the polymer, it increases the absorption of quercetin and decreases the fluorescence intensity (maximum ΔF). However, at pH values higher than 8.0 and under strong alkaline conditions, due to the electrostatic repulsion between the negative charges on the surface of quercetin and the adsorbent, and on the other hand, the possibility of nucleophilic attack on the surface of the adsorbent by hydroxyl groups, the interaction between the adsorbent and the analyte molecule is affected and as a result, the binding stability of quercetin molecules at the binding sites in MIP decreases, which leads to a decrease in the amount of quercetin absorption and followed by a decrease in the fluorescence signal (ΔF). Therefore, pH 8.0 was considered as the optimal pH in subsequent experiments.

To evaluate the effect of contact time, 300 μL of quercetin solution (100 ng mL−1, pH 8.0) was mixed with 300 μL of adsorbent (0.6 mg mL−1) and contacted at various time intervals of 1 to 30 min (Fig. 3c). Then the fluorescence emission signal was recorded. A rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed at the initial 5 min and then after 10 min, fluorescence intensity changes were fixed. Because the lifetime of fluorescence is short and the fluorescence emission decreases with the passage of time until it reaches a constant value. Therefore, we included 10 min as the optimal contact time in subsequent experiments.

To study the ability to detection quercetin by Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP, experiments were performed experiments were performed at different concentrations of quercetin in the range of 5–220 ng mL−1. Experiments have shown that a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP can be observed by increasing the concentration of quercetin even in small amounts. This reduction was proportional to the quercetin concentration, so that a lower fluorescence intensity was obtained when high concentrations of quercetin were used by Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP. Therefore, a linear measurement diagram of changes in the variation of fluorescence emission signal (ΔF) versus quercetin concentration in the linear range (LR) of 5–220 ng mL−1 (ΔF = 0.0055C + 0.0115; R2 = 0.9998) was drawn using Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP (Fig. 3d). As can be seen from the figure, the sensitivity of the signals obtained for the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP was higher than that of the Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP, indicating the synthesis of a porous adsorbent with effective imprinting for the quercetin molecules. Then, for the data related to Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP adsorbent, the limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantitation (LOQ), imprinting factor (IF) and recovery were calculated, which were 0.54 ng mL−1, 1.81 ng mL−1, 4.23 and 99.73(%) respectively. Also, the repeatability (RSD) of the method by measuring three times of quercetin with a concentration of 100 ng mL−1 obtained an acceptable number of 1.78.

A comparison of the efficiency of the quercetin detection method using adsorbent Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP with other reported adsorbents and analytical methods in the sources has been brought in Table 1 . The presented results show the satisfactory detection limit and detection power of quercetin by the proposed adsorbent in comparison with other adsorbents and analytical methods. Also, the synthesis process in this work is easy and fast and does not require additional pre-treatment steps, and many of the principles of GAC, such as low consumption amounts, low cost of materials and the use of solvents and low-risk chemicals, have been observed. The use of Fe3O4 nanoparticles became a suitable alternative for centrifuges and various filtration systems in separation. As a result, the proposed system was a new green magnetic MIP-based fluorescent adsorbent for the determination of quercetin, which was successfully used to determine low amounts of quercetin in plant samples.

The binding ability and selectivity parameters were investigated using the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP. The selectivity test was performed for quercetin, gallic acid and caffeine molecules with the same concentration of 100 ng mL−1. For this purpose, first the change in fluorescence intensity of quercetin, gallic acid and caffeine molecules was measured with Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP and Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP, then the extracted concentration was measured with using the line equation of the calibration graph (Fig. 3d) was calculated. After that, the adsorption capacity was calculated by Eq (1), the results are shown in Fig. 4b, as shown in the figure, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP for quercetin is much higher than gallic acid and caffeine, however, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP for quercetin, gallic acid and caffeine is not much different from each other. In this way, it can be concluded that the imprinted sites are complementary to the quercetin in terms of shape, size and functional groups, which can cause a significant quenching in Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP fluorescence intensity, but gallic acid and caffeine with a different shape, molecular weight and functional group than quercetin, are not compatible with the imprinted sites and had a lower chance of fluorescence quenching. In the next part, selectivity parameters, α and β were calculated according to Eq (2) and (3) using the obtained adsorption capacity. The results are presented in Table 3 .
(1)
Q
MIP , NIP
=
C ext
V analyte
/ m
(2)
IF
α
=
Q MIP
/
Q NIP
SF
β
=
α template
/
α analog

In the Eq: (1), Q (ng mg−1) is the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP or Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP, C (ng mL−1) is the extracted concentration of quercetin from the calibration line equation, V (mL) is the volume of quercetin solution and m (mg) is the weight of the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP or Fe3O4@GQDs/NIP. In the Eq: (2) α is the imprinting factor. In the Eq: (3), β is the selectivity factor, αtemplate is the imprinting factor of quercetin and αanalog is the imprinting factor of gallic acid or caffeine.

The quantitative results for the quercetin determination in thyme, cumin and green tea samples has been shown in Table 4
. The method accuracy was evaluated by adding certain amounts of standard quercetin solution to the real samples matrix. The recovery percentage values were in the satisfactory range 97.61–102.11(%). Also, the accuracy and repeatability (RSD) of the method for three-time consecutive measurements of real samples was obtained in a satisfactory range of 1.18–3.71(%). The results indicate that the proposed adsorbent Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP extracted quercetin well from real samples.

In this work, we prepared Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP through the self-assembly reaction of APTES and TEOS polymerization precursors and quercetin molecule as a template, for fluorescent determination of quercetin in green tea, thyme and cumin samples. The synthesis process was fast, simple, green and economical. First GQDs was prepared by thermal pyrolysis of glucose and then after magnetization by Fe3O4 nanoparticle, was coated with a layer of MIP. After effective washing to remove the templet molecules, the residual imprinting site acted as a special acceptor and had the ability to absorb quercetin from soluble samples with appropriate selectivity. GQDs have many advantages over semiconductor QDs, like resistant fluorescence, little toxicity and suitable water solubility. Among these properties, the fluorescence emission is the most prominent property of GQDs which has been used in this work. The presence of quercetin in the imprinting sites, causes the quenching of fluorescence emission. According to the Stern-Volmer equation, fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with concentration. Therefore, we can use the drop of the fluorescence emission signal (ΔF) for quantitative measurement quercetin. This method was able to detect the concentration of quercetin in the range of 5–220 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.54 ng mL−1 and a recovery percentage of 97.61–102.11(%).The selectivity and affinity of the MIP adsorbent to the template molecule, and on the other hand, the low toxicity and high fluorescence of GQD, have turned the Fe3O4@GQDs/MIP into a smart material that can be used in GAC. Due to the presence of phenolic compounds including quercetin (with valuable antioxidant and antiviral properties) in many commonly used medicinal plants and spices, it is important to present a method for the synthesis of a green adsorbent with high selectivity and sensitivity for the extraction and measurement of quercetin.

Nanotechnology and nanoparticles (CQD and GQD), if based on green and environmental chemistry, are expected to have a great impact in a new field called “green nanomedicine”. The superior chemical and photochemical stability of GQDs, good solubility in water as well as their low toxicity make GQDs attractive candidates for biomedical applications. Green nanomedicine delivery systems based on safe chemical reactions for the environment and the use of natural biological materials such as plant extracts to produce innovative materials create a revolution in this field. Also, in the coming decades, MIPs will be used not only in precise molecular recognition, but also in response to stimuli such as temperature, pH, light, electric/magnetic fields, and solute concentration. To achieve such goals, it is necessary to combine MIP with other functional materials. Moreover, it can be predicted that MIP-based materials will be used for drug delivery system, cell culture, etc., apart from simple analytical applications, in the near future. Therefore, future research should mainly focus on further searching for new applications for GQDs and combining them with more MIPs in the field of green chemistry.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the University of Kurdistan for all the support it has provided in all aspects of the above project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108233.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
